details.text: Derivative: وَ – The وَاوُ القَسْم (Waw ul-Qasm – Waw of the oath) was used by the Arabs in the Arabic language as a style to grab the attention of those being addressed. When Allah uses an oath to swear by something, He intends for us to reflect upon the thing the oath is being sworn upon to prepare us for the lessons of the Surah.

Derivative: الْفَجْرِ – All words derived from فَجَرَ relate to something splitting, cleaving or bursting open or through. الْفَجْرِ depicts when light tears through the night. The first instance or sign of that light cracking through is الْفَجْرِ (Al-Fajr).

Derivative: الْأَوْتَادِ means the pegs that hold tents and can be used to refer to tents in general. It is an expression of the army and military that strengthens Fir’awn’s kingdom just like pegs strengthen tents.

Derivative: طَغَوْا is from the verb طَغَى – يَطْغُو - طُغْيان which means to rebel against and exceed the proper limits. Here, طَغَوْا means that they committed excesses in terms of acts of oppression, sinful acts and great crimes.
Derivative: الْفَسَادَ refers to corruption and deterioration.

Derivative: سَوْطَ literally means a whip which is used to strike the skin of a criminal as a punishment and was regarded to be the worst and most painful form of punishment. Here it is being used to describe the instrument of Allah’s punishment illustrating a quick and painful punishment that will be poured upon them.
Derivative: صَبَّ means to pour out all at once

Derivative: الْمِرْصَادِ in origin refers to a place that someone waits in to observe or see a particular thing. It is used to refer to the place where someone is lying in wait to undertake an ambush. Here it means that Allah is observing and monitoring all actions of his servants and that nothing escapes Him.

Derivative: أَكْرَمَ means to provide honour to someone or bestow honour upon him. This can imply being given a good reputation, standing and a position of honour in his society in addition to an abundance in wealth.
Derivative: نَعَّمَ means to provide abundance of favours that provide ease and comfort in life.
Derivative: اِبْتَلَى means to test with a difficult and trying test or trial.

Derivative: قَدَرَ in origin relates to calculating a certain amount of something and is used here to imply that only a certain amount of Rizq will reach the person i.e., it will not be abundant and be constrained. قَدَرَ has been said to mean ضَيَّقَ here which means to tighten and constrict.

Derivative: لَمًّا comes from لَمَّ which means to collect and gather together. In reference to the inheritance and the statement أَكْلًا لَمًّا, it implies that you want to consume all of it without consideration for others.

Derivative: التُّرَاثَ comes from وَرِثَ - وِرَاثَة which means to inherit. التُّرَاثَ refers to the wealth that is inherited and can also refer to any money that comes to you without doing any work for it.

وَلَيَالٍ عَشْرٍ

- وَلَيَالٍ عَشْرٍ “And by (or I swear by) ten nights” – The scholars of Tafsir have said this refers to the ten nights of the first ten of Dhu l-Hijjah or the last ten nights of Ramadan.
Derivative: جَمًّا is used in Arabic to indicate to abundance or plenitude.
Derivative: دُكَّتِ from دَكَّ – دَكًّا means to be flattened, pounded to dust or to be demolished and levelled.

Derivative: قَدَّمْتُ
Reference: قَدَّمْتُ means to send forward and carries the meaning of to invest.
- يَا لَيْتَ is an expression of the greatest level of regret said in a state of abject despair: “Oh, if only I had done such and such, I wouldn’t have been in such ruin”.
- لِحَيَاتِي means “For my life” and refers here to the life of the hereafter.

Derivative: الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ comes from the verb اِطْمَأَنَّ – يَطْمَئِنُّ – اِطْمِئْنَانًا and it means to been completely tranquil and in a state of inner peace. مُطْمَئِنّ is the one who is such a state of tranquillity, peace and calm.
- “In a state of being pleased or satisfied and being the recipient of pleasure or satisfaction” i.e., pleased and satisfied with what Allah gives you and in receipt of Allah’s pleasure.
Derivative: الْوَتْرِ refers to that which is by itself or singular which can be translated as “the odd” as opposed to “the even” (الشَّفْعِ).
Derivative: الشَّفْعِ refers to that which is the second to other than it i.e., that which joins or is added to something else to make it paired or even.
Derivative: يَسْرِ from سَرَى – يَسْرِي which means to take a walk or to travel at nighttime and in respect to the night it can mean to pass along until it nears its end and reaches the day.

Derivative: حِجْرٍ carries the meaning of being forbidden or interdicted i.e., something that constrains. It was used by the Arabs to refer to the عَقْل (‘Aql – mind or intellect) because the mind forbids and constrains the person from that which he shouldn’t do. The one possessing حِجْرٍ (Hijr) is the one who possesses a sound intellect and reasoning (‘Aql).

Derivative: تَرَ is from رأَى – يَرَى – رَأْيٌ or رُؤْيَةٌ which means “to see” and it is said to be from the actions of the heart i.e., type of sight that implies thinking, considering and reflecting upon something.
Derivative: إِرَمَ refers to the name of the original tribe of ‘Aad (عَادٍ) i.e., whom the lineage of ‘Aad goes back to.
Derivative: الْعِمَادِ means pillars which is an expression of their might and strength.
Derivative: الْبِلَادِ – بِلاد (Bilaad) is the plural of بَلَد (Balad) and is used to refer to a land which has defined borders.
Derivative: الصَّخْرَ refers to large solid rocks, boulders and rock formations.

Derivative: جَابُوا means to penetrate, cut and bore through something. Here it refers to carving through and giving shape and form to rock which implies a much heavier and more powerful process than carving through wood.
